stimulus: A student has taken twelve courses and received a B in a majority of them. The student is now taking another course and will probably, given her record, receive a B in it.
questionStem: Each of the following, if true, strengthens the argument except:

answerChoices.A: The student previously studied alone but is receiving help from several outstanding students during the present course.
answerChoices.B: The twelve courses together covered a broad range of subject matter.
answerChoices.C: The student previously studied in the library and continues to do so.
answerChoices.D: The student received a B in all but one of the twelve courses.
answerChoices.E: The current course is a continuation of one of the twelve courses in which the student received a B.

stimulus: If the government increases its funding for civilian scientific research, private patrons and industries will believe that such research has become primarily the government’s responsibility. When they believe that research is no longer primarily their responsibility, private patrons and industries will decrease their contributions toward research. Therefore, in order to keep from depressing the overall level of funding for civilian scientific research, the government should not increase its own funding.
questionStem: Which one of the following is an assumption on which the argument relies?

answerChoices.A: Governments should bear the majority of the financial burden of funding for civilian scientific research.
answerChoices.B: Any increase in government funding would displace more private funding for civilian scientific research than it would provide.
answerChoices.C: Private donations toward research are no longer welcomed by researchers whose work receives government funding.
answerChoices.D: Civilian scientific research cannot be conducted efficiently with more than one source of funding.
answerChoices.E: Funding for civilian scientific research is currently at the highest possible level.

stimulus: For any given ticket in a 1000-ticket lottery, it is reasonable to believe that that ticket will lose. Hence, it is reasonable to believe that no ticket will win.
questionStem: Which one of the following exhibits flawed reasoning most similar to the flawed reasoning in the argument above?

answerChoices.A: It is reasonable to believe for any randomly drawn playing card that it will not be an ace, so it is reasonable to believe that an ace will never be drawn.
answerChoices.B: When the chances of a certain horse winning the race are 999 out of 1000, it is reasonable to believe that that horse will win. So it is reasonable to believe that no one other than that horse can win.
answerChoices.C: It is unreasonable to believe that 1000 consecutive coin flips will turn up heads, so it is reasonable to believe that this never happens.
answerChoices.D: It is reasonable to believe that if the most recent flip of a given coin was tails, the next flip will be heads. So if a coin has turned up tails the last 1000 times it was flipped, it is reasonable to believe that it will turn up heads the next time it is flipped.
answerChoices.E: For any given group of five-year-old children, the average height is one meter, so it is reasonable to believe that if Pat is five years old, she is exactly one meter tall.

stimulus: Dental researcher: Filling a cavity in a tooth is not a harmless procedure: it inevitably damages some of the healthy parts of the tooth. Cavities are harmful only if the decay reaches the nerves inside the tooth, and many cavities, if left untreated, never progress to that point. Therefore, dentists should not fill a cavity unless the nerves inside the tooth are in imminent danger from that cavity.
questionStem: Which one of the following principles, if valid, most strongly supports the researcher’s reasoning?

answerChoices.A: Dentists should perform any procedure that is likely to be beneficial in the long term, but only if the procedure does not cause immediate damage.
answerChoices.B: Dentists should help their patients to prevent cavities rather than waiting until cavities are present to begin treatment.
answerChoices.C: A condition that is only potentially harmful should not be treated using a method that is definitely harmful.
answerChoices.D: A condition that is typically progressive should not be treated using methods that provide only temporary relief.
answerChoices.E: A condition that is potentially harmful should not be left untreated unless it can be kept under constant surveillance.

stimulus: The number of codfish in the North Atlantic has declined substantially as the population of harp seals has increased from two million to more than three million. Some blame the seal for the shrinking cod population, but cod plays a negligible role in the seal’s diet. It is therefore unlikely that the increase in the seal population has contributed significantly to the decline in the cod population.
questionStem: Which one of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument?

answerChoices.A: People who fish for cod commercially are inconvenienced by the presence of large numbers of seals near traditional fishing grounds.
answerChoices.B: Water pollution poses a more serious threat to cod than to the harp seal.
answerChoices.C: The harp seal thrives in water that is too cold to support a dense population of cod.
answerChoices.D: Cod feed almost exclusively on capelin, a fish that is a staple of the harp seal’s diet.
answerChoices.E: The cod population in the North Atlantic began to decline before the harp-seal population began to increase.

stimulus:
The following passage is used for Section 1, questions 6 through 7.
Hospital auditor: The Rodríguez family stipulated that the funds they donated to the neurological clinic all be used to minimize patients’ suffering. The clinic administration is clearly violating those terms, since it has allocated nearly one fifth of those funds for research into new diagnostic technologies, instead of letting that money flow directly to its patients.
Clinic administrator: But the successful development of new technologies will allow early diagnosis of many neurological disorders. In most cases, patients who are treated in the early stages of neurological disorders suffer far less than do patients who are not treated until their neurological disorders reach advanced stages.
questionStem: Which one of the following is the main point at issue between the hospital auditor and the clinic administrator?

answerChoices.A: whether early treatment of many neurological disorders lessens the suffering associated with those disorders rather than completely eliminating such suffering
answerChoices.B: whether the patients being treated at the neurological clinic are currently receiving adequate treatment for the neurological disorders from which they suffer
answerChoices.C: whether the Rodríguez family clearly stipulated that the funds they donated to the neurological clinic be used to minimize patients’ suffering
answerChoices.D: whether the neurological clinic is adhering strictly to the conditions the Rodríguez family placed on the allocation of the funds they donated to the clinic
answerChoices.E: whether the Rodríguez family anticipated that some of the funds they donated to the neurological clinic would be used to pay for research into new diagnostic technologies

stimulus:
The following passage is used for Section 1, questions 6 through 7.
Hospital auditor: The Rodríguez family stipulated that the funds they donated to the neurological clinic all be used to minimize patients’ suffering. The clinic administration is clearly violating those terms, since it has allocated nearly one fifth of those funds for research into new diagnostic technologies, instead of letting that money flow directly to its patients.
Clinic administrator: But the successful development of new technologies will allow early diagnosis of many neurological disorders. In most cases, patients who are treated in the early stages of neurological disorders suffer far less than do patients who are not treated until their neurological disorders reach advanced stages.
questionStem: The clinic administrator responds to the hospital auditor by doing which one of the following?

answerChoices.A: demonstrating that the hospital auditor’s conclusion, though broadly correct, stands in need of a minor qualification
answerChoices.B: showing that the hospital auditor’s argument fails to separate what is the case from what ought to be the case
answerChoices.C: reminding the hospital auditor that, in the case at issue, being told what to do is tantamount to being told how to do it
answerChoices.D: arguing that, in assessing the severity of a violation, the reasoning motivating the violation needs to be considered
answerChoices.E: reinterpreting a key phrase in the hospital auditor’s argument so as to undermine an assumption underlying that argument

stimulus: Generally speaking, if the same crop is sown in a field for several successive years, growth in the later years is poorer than growth in the earlier years, since nitrogen in the soil becomes depleted. Even though alfalfa is a nitrogen-fixing plant and thus increases the amount of nitrogen in the soil, surprisingly, it too, if planted in the same field year after year, grows less well in the later years than it does in the earlier years.
questionStem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to explain the similarity described above between alfalfa and non-nitrogen-fixing plants?

answerChoices.A: Some kinds of plants grow more rapidly and are more productive when they are grown among other kinds of plants rather than being grown only among plants of their own kind.
answerChoices.B: Alfalfa increases the amount of nitrogen in the soil by taking nitrogen from the air and releasing it in a form that is usable by most kinds of plants.
answerChoices.C: Certain types of plants, including alfalfa, produce substances that accumulate in the soil and that are toxic to the plants that produce those substances.
answerChoices.D: Alfalfa increases nitrogen in the soil in which it grows only if a certain type of soil bacteria is present in the soil.
answerChoices.E: Alfalfa is very sensitive to juglone, a compound that is exuded from the leaves of black walnut trees.

stimulus: Political commentators see recent policies of the government toward Country X as appeasement, pure and simple. This view is fundamentally mistaken, for polls show that most people disagree with the political commentators’ assessment of government policies toward Country X.
questionStem: The reasoning in the argument is questionable because

answerChoices.A: the term “policies” is used ambiguously in the argument
answerChoices.B: the political commentators discussed in the passage are not identified
answerChoices.C: a claim is inferred to be false merely because a majority of people believe it to be false
answerChoices.D: the claim that the political commentators are mistaken is both a premise and a conclusion in the argument
answerChoices.E: it is assumed that what is true of persons individually is true of a country as a whole

stimulus: It is a principle of economics that a nation can experience economic growth only when consumer confidence is balanced with a small amount of consumer skepticism.
questionStem: Which one of the following is an application of the economic principle above?

answerChoices.A: Any nation in which consumer confidence is balanced with a small amount of consumer skepticism will experience economic growth.
answerChoices.B: Any nation in which the prevailing attitude of consumers is not skepticism will experience economic growth.
answerChoices.C: Any nation in which the prevailing attitude of consumers is either exclusively confidence or exclusively skepticism will experience economic growth.
answerChoices.D: Any nation in which the prevailing attitude of consumers is exclusively confidence will not experience economic growth.
answerChoices.E: Any nation in which consumer skepticism is balanced with a small amount of consumer confidence will experience economic growth.

stimulus: Sharks have a higher ratio of cartilage mass to body mass than any other organism. They also have a greater resistance to cancer than any other organism. Shark cartilage contains a substance that inhibits tumor growth by stopping the development of a new blood network. In the past 20 years, none of the responses among terminal cancer patients to various therapeutic measures has been more positive than the response among those who consumed shark cartilage.
questionStem: If the claims made above are true, then each of the following could be true except:

answerChoices.A: No organism resists cancer better than sharks do, but some resist cancer as well as sharks.
answerChoices.B: The organism most susceptible to cancer has a higher percentage of cartilage than some organisms that are less susceptible to cancer.
answerChoices.C: The substance in shark cartilage that inhibits tumor growth is found in most organisms.
answerChoices.D: In the past 20 years many terminal cancer patients have improved dramatically following many sorts of therapy.
answerChoices.E: Some organisms have immune systems more efficient than a shark’s immune system.

stimulus:
The following passage is used for Section 1, questions 12 through 13.
People who say that Dooney County is flat are clearly wrong. On flat land, soil erosion by water is not a problem. Consequently, farmers whose land is flat do not build terraces to prevent erosion. Yet I hear that the farms in Dooney County are dotted with terraces.
questionStem: The author’s conclusion in the passage depends on the assumption that

answerChoices.A: the only cause of soil erosion is water
answerChoices.B: there are terraces on farmland in Dooney County which were built to prevent soil erosion
answerChoices.C: terraces of the kind found on farmland in Dooney County have been shown to prevent soil erosion
answerChoices.D: on flat land there is no soil erosion
answerChoices.E: the only terraces in Dooney County are on farmland

stimulus:
The following passage is used for Section 1, questions 12 through 13.
People who say that Dooney County is flat are clearly wrong. On flat land, soil erosion by water is not a problem. Consequently, farmers whose land is flat do not build terraces to prevent erosion. Yet I hear that the farms in Dooney County are dotted with terraces.
questionStem: The reasoning in the passage is most similar to that in which one of the following?

answerChoices.A: If we paint the room white, it will get smudged, and we will have to paint it again soon. Therefore, we should paint it dark blue.
answerChoices.B: People with children need more space than those without children. Yet people with no children can usually afford bigger houses.
answerChoices.C: People who get a lot of exercise have no trouble falling asleep; hence, people who get a lot of exercise do not use medication to help them fall asleep. Jack is taking many kinds of medication, so he must not be getting a lot of exercise.
answerChoices.D: If I go grocery shopping when I am hungry, I buy snack foods and cannot resist eating them. Therefore, I cannot lose weight.
answerChoices.E: People who have many friends tend to go out often, so they need cars. Therefore, if Joe wants to have many friends, he must buy a car.

stimulus: The axis of Earth’s daily rotation is tilted with respect to the plane of its orbit at an angle of roughly 23 degrees. That angle can be kept fairly stable only by the gravitational influence of Earth’s large, nearby Moon. Without such a stable and moderate axis tilt, a planet’s climate is too extreme and unstable to support life. Mars, for example, has only very small moons, tilts at wildly fluctuating angles, and cannot support life.
questionStem: If the statements above are true, which one of the following must also be true on the basis of them?

answerChoices.A: If Mars had a sufficiently large nearby moon, Mars would be able to support life.
answerChoices.B: If Earth’s Moon were to leave Earth’s orbit, Earth’s climate would be unable to support life.
answerChoices.C: Any planet with a stable, moderate axis tilt can support life.
answerChoices.D: Gravitational influences other than moons have little or no effect on the magnitude of the tilt angle of either Earth’s or Mars’s axis.
answerChoices.E: No planet that has more than one moon can support life.

stimulus: The town of Springhill frequently must declare a water emergency, making it temporarily unlawful to use water for such nonessential purposes as car washing. These emergencies could be avoided if Springhill would introduce permanent economic incentives for water conservation. Actually, Springhill discourages conservation because each household pays a modest monthly flat fee for any amount of water below a certain usage threshold, and a substantial per-liter rate only after the threshold is reached.
questionStem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?

answerChoices.A: The Springhill authorities do a poor job of enforcing its water emergency laws and many people break the laws without incurring a penalty.
answerChoices.B: The town council of Springhill recently refused to raise the threshold.
answerChoices.C: The threshold is kept at a high enough level to exceed the water requirements of most households in Springhill.
answerChoices.D: The threshold is not as high in Springhill as it is in neighboring towns.
answerChoices.E: The threshold remains at the predetermined level specified by law until a change is approved by the Springhill town council.

stimulus: Poppy petals function to attract pollinating insects. The pollination of a poppy flower triggers the release into that flower of a substance that causes its petals to wilt within one or two days. If the flower is not pollinated, the substance will not be released and the petals will remain fresh for a week or longer, as long as the plant can nourish them. Cutting an unpollinated poppy flower from the plant triggers the release into the flower of the same substance whose release is triggered by pollination.
questionStem: The statements above, if true, most strongly support which one of the following?

answerChoices.A: Pollinating insects are not attracted to wilted poppy flowers.
answerChoices.B: Even if cut poppies are given all necessary nutrients, their petals will tend to wilt within a few days.
answerChoices.C: Flowers of all plants release the substance that causes wilting when they are cut, although the amount released may vary.
answerChoices.D: The pollen on pollinated poppy flowers prevents their petals from absorbing the nutrients carried to them by their stems.
answerChoices.E: Poppy plants are unable to draw nutrients from soil or water after the substance that causes wilting has been released.

stimulus:
When a community opens a large shopping mall, it often expects a boost to the local economy, and in fact a large amount of economic activity goes on in these malls. Yet the increase in the local economy is typically much smaller than the total amount of economic activity that
goes on in the mall.
questionStem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to explain the discrepancy described above?

answerChoices.A: When large shopping malls are new they attract a lot of shoppers but once the novelty has worn off they usually attract fewer shoppers than does the traditional downtown shopping district.
answerChoices.B: Most of the money spent in a large shopping mall is spent by tourists who are drawn specifically by the mall and who would not have visited the community had that mall not been built.
answerChoices.C: Most of the jobs created by large shopping malls are filled by people who recently moved to the community and who would not have moved had there been no job offer in the community.
answerChoices.D: Most of the money spent in a large shopping mall is money that would have been spent elsewhere in the same community had that mall not been built.
answerChoices.E: Most of the jobs created by the construction of a large shopping mall are temporary, and most of the permanent jobs created are low paying.

stimulus: Essayist: The way science is conducted and regulated can be changed. But we need to determine whether the changes are warranted, taking into account their price. The use of animals in research could end immediately, but only at the cost of abandoning many kinds of research and making others very expensive. The use of recombinant DNA could be drastically curtailed. Many other restrictions could be imposed, complete with a system of fraud police. But such massive interventions would be costly and would change the character of science.
questionStem: Which one of the following most accurately expresses the main conclusion of the essayist’s argument?

answerChoices.A: We should not make changes that will alter the character of science.
answerChoices.B: If we regulate science more closely, we will change the character of science.
answerChoices.C: The regulation of science and the conducting of science can be changed.
answerChoices.D: The imposition of restrictions on the conduct of science would be very costly.
answerChoices.E: We need to be aware of the impact of change in science before changes are made.

stimulus: The postmodern view involves the rejection of modern assumptions about order and the universality of truth. The grand theories of the modern era are now seen as limited by the social and historical contexts in which they were elaborated. Also, the belief in order has given way to a belief in the importance of irregularity and chaos. It follows that we inhabit a world full of irregular events, and in which there are no universal truths.
questionStem: The argument’s reasoning is questionable because the argument

answerChoices.A: infers that something is the case because it is believed to be the case
answerChoices.B: uses the term “universal” ambiguously
answerChoices.C: relies on the use of emotional terms to bolster its conclusion
answerChoices.D: uses the term “order” ambiguously
answerChoices.E: fails to cite examples of modern theories that purport to embody universal truths

stimulus: If the economy is weak, then prices remain constant although unemployment rises. But unemployment rises only if investment decreases. Fortunately, investment is not decreasing.
questionStem: If the statements above are true, then which one of the following must be false?

answerChoices.A: Either the economy is weak or investment is decreasing.
answerChoices.B: If unemployment rises, then prices remain constant.
answerChoices.C: The economy is weak only if investment decreases.
answerChoices.D: Either the economy is weak or prices are remaining constant.
answerChoices.E: Either unemployment is rising or the economy is not weak.

stimulus: Psychologist: Some astrologers claim that our horoscopes completely determine our personalities, but this claim is false. I concede that identical twins— who are, of course, born at practically the same time—often do have similar personalities. However, birth records were examined to find two individuals who were born 40 years ago on the same day and at exactly the same time—one in a hospital in Toronto and one in a hospital in New York. Personality tests revealed that the personalities of these two individuals are in fact different.
questionStem: Which one of the following is an assumption on which the psychologist’s argument depends?

answerChoices.A: Astrologers have not subjected their claims to rigorous experimentation.
answerChoices.B: The personality differences between the two individuals cannot be explained by the cultural differences between Toronto and New York.
answerChoices.C: The geographical difference between Toronto and New York did not result in the two individuals having different horoscopes.
answerChoices.D: Complete birth records for the past 40 years were kept at both hospitals.
answerChoices.E: Identical twins have identical genetic structures and usually have similar home environments.

stimulus: Under the influence of today’s computer-oriented culture, publishing for children has taken on a flashy new look that emphasizes illustrations and graphic design; the resulting lack of substance leads to books that are shortlived items covering mainly trendy subjects. The changes also include more humorous content, simplification of difficult material, and a narrower focus on specific topics.
questionStem: Which one of the following is most strongly supported by the information above?

answerChoices.A: The inclusion of humorous material and a narrower focus detract from the substance of a children’s book.
answerChoices.B: The substance of a children’s book is important to its longevity.
answerChoices.C: Children of the computer generation cannot concentrate on long, unbroken sections of prose.
answerChoices.D: Children judge books primarily on the basis of graphic design.
answerChoices.E: The lack of substance of a children’s book is unlikely to be important to its popularity.

stimulus: Further evidence of a connection between brain physiology and psychological states has recently been uncovered in the form of a correlation between electroencephalograph patterns and characteristic moods. A study showed that participants who suffered from clinical depression exhibited less left frontal lobe activity than right, while, conversely, characteristically goodnatured participants exhibited greater left lobe activity. Thus one’s general disposition is a result of the activity of one’s frontal lobe.
questionStem: Each of the following, if true, weakens the argument except:

answerChoices.A: Many drugs prescribed to combat clinical depression act by causing increased left lobe activity.
answerChoices.B: Excessive sleep, a typical consequence of clinical depression, is known to suppress left lobe activity.
answerChoices.C: Frontal lobe activity is not subject to variation the way general disposition is.
answerChoices.D: Earlier studies indicated that frontal lobe activity and emotive states are both caused by activity in the brain’s limbic system.
answerChoices.E: Social interaction of the kind not engaged in by most clinically depressed people is known to stimulate left lobe activity.

stimulus: We ought to pay attention only to the intrinsic properties of a work of art. Its other, extrinsic properties are irrelevant to our aesthetic interactions with it. For example, when we look at a painting we should consider only what is directly presented in our experience of it. What is really aesthetically relevant, therefore, is not what a painting symbolizes, but what it directly presents to experience.
questionStem: The conclusion follows logically if which one of the following is added to the premises?

answerChoices.A: What an artwork symbolizes involves only extrinsic properties of that work.
answerChoices.B: There are certain properties of our experiences of artworks that can be distinguished as symbolic properties.
answerChoices.C: Only an artwork’s intrinsic properties are relevant to our aesthetic interactions with it.
answerChoices.D: It is possible in theory for an artwork to symbolize nothing.
answerChoices.E: An intrinsic property of an artwork is one that relates the work to itself.

stimulus:
McKinley: A double-blind study, in which neither the patient nor the primary researcher knows whether the patient is being given the drug being tested or a placebo, is the most effective procedure for testing the efficacy of a drug. But we will not be able to perform such a study on this new drug, since the drug will have various effects on the patients’ bodies, which will make us aware of whether the patients are getting the drug or a placebo. Engle: You cannot draw that conclusion at this point, for
you are assuming you know what the outcome of the study will be.
questionStem: Engle’s statement indicates that he is most likely interpreting McKinley’s remarks to be

answerChoices.A: presuming that a double-blind study is the only effective way to test new drugs
answerChoices.B: denying that the drug will be effective
answerChoices.C: presuming that the placebo will produce no effects whatever on the patients’ bodies
answerChoices.D: referring to the drug’s therapeutic effects rather than to any known side effects
answerChoices.E: based on a confusion about when a drug is efficacious

stimulus: Modern navigation systems, which are found in most of today’s commercial aircraft, are made with low-power circuitry, which is more susceptible to interference than the vacuum-tube circuitry found in older planes. During landing, navigation systems receive radio signals from the airport to guide the plane to the runway. Recently, one plane with low-power circuitry veered off course during landing, its dials dimming, when a passenger turned on a laptop computer. Clearly, modern aircraft navigation systems are being put at risk by the electronic devices that passengers carry on board, such as cassette players and laptop computers.
questionStem: Which one of the following, if true, least strengthens the argument above?

answerChoices.A: After the laptop computer was turned off, the plane regained course and its navigation instruments and dials returned to normal.
answerChoices.B: When in use all electronic devices emit electromagnetic radiation, which is known to interfere with circuitry.
answerChoices.C: No problems with navigational equipment or instrument dials have been reported on flights with no passenger-owned electronic devices on board.
answerChoices.D: Significant electromagnetic radiation from portable electronic devices can travel up to eight meters, and some passenger seats on modern aircraft are located within four meters of the navigation systems.
answerChoices.E: Planes were first equipped with low-power circuitry at about the same time portable electronic devices became popular.
